18: A direct revelation: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself: I am YHWH.' Jesus elevates Leviticus 19:18 to the second-greatest commandment (Matthew 22:39) and Paul calls it the fulfillment of the whole law (Galatians 5:14; Romans 13:9). But Christ does not merely cite the command — he embodies it: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13). The neighbor-love of Leviticus 19:18 reaches its ultimate expression in the cross, where Christ loved his neighbors — his enemies — at the cost of his life (Romans 5:8: God demonstrates his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us).
1: A literary frame: the formula 'YHWH spoke to Moses, saying' opens the Holiness Code (Leviticus 17-26), the dense heart of Leviticus. Every commandment that follows is YHWH's direct speech — the law is not a human ethical system but the expression of the divine character. Christ as the eternal Word (John 1:1) is the personal embodiment of that character: 'the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17). The Holiness Code's 'I am YHWH' refrain (occurring ~15 times in Leviticus 19 alone) grounds every obligation in the divine self-disclosure that reaches its fullness in the incarnate Son.
2: A direct quotation: 'You shall be holy, for I YHWH your God am holy.' This is the Holiness Code's governing mandate, and 1 Peter 1:15-16 quotes it directly as the standard for the new covenant community: 'as he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct.' The ground of the command shifts in 1 Peter: it is the Father who calls, but the means is 'the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or spot' (1 Peter 1:19). Christ himself is YHWH's holiness made visible — 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Hebrews 7:26); his people are holy by union with him.
3: A shadow: 'Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father, and keep my sabbaths.' Christ himself lived this commandment — twelve years old in the temple yet returning to Nazareth to be subject to Mary and Joseph (Luke 2:51). Yet he also reorders the honor-command in light of ultimate allegiance: 'Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me' (Matthew 10:37). In Christ the fifth commandment reaches its telos — not the abolition of family honor but its relativization within the kingdom family, where the deepest bond is to the Father and to brothers and sisters in Christ.
4: A shadow: 'Do not turn to idols or make for yourselves cast metal images of gods.' Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' (Colossians 1:15) — the one in whom humanity can truly see God without distortion. The prohibition of cast images is the negative space pointing toward the positive reality: the invisible God, unable to be represented in wood or metal, chose to represent himself in the flesh of the Son. 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9). The idol-prohibition is the shadow of the incarnation — no human-made image will suffice; only the image God himself made in his Son.
5: A shadow: the fellowship offering regulations open with the requirement that the sacrifice be offered 'for your acceptance' (lirtsonchem) — offered in the way that will be pleasing to YHWH. Christ's offering is the perfect fulfillment: 'he gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2). The phrase 'fragrant aroma' (reyach nichoach) applied to the fellowship offering becomes Paul's description of Christ's death — the self-offering that is maximally pleasing to the Father, the sacrifice YHWH accepts in the fullest sense.
6: A shadow: flesh left from the fellowship offering past the second day 'shall be burned with fire; it is tainted (pigul).' The time-limit on eating the sacrifice means the celebration of restored fellowship has its appointed season — it cannot be stored up indefinitely. Christ's resurrection breaks the decay-pattern: 'you will not allow your Holy One to see corruption' (Psalms 16:10; Acts 2:27). The fellowship meal in Christ is not time-limited; his body does not decay, and the eucharistic feast he inaugurates will find its fullness at the messianic banquet in the age to come.
7: A shadow: the tainted offering eaten on the third day shall not be accepted. The number three echoes in the resurrection narrative: 'after three days he will rise' (Matthew 27:63). The rejected offering that is eaten beyond its proper time is not accepted — by contrast, Christ's offering, consumed and completed within the proper time (he rose on the third day, not after), is maximally accepted. The sacrifice that fulfilled its purpose in its proper moment is the antithesis of the pigul — the offering YHWH accepted and raised.
8: A shadow: eating the tainted offering means 'that person shall bear his iniquity.' The phrase nasa avon (bear iniquity) is the same language used of the suffering servant: 'he bore the sin of many' (Isaiah 53:12). What the offerer incurs by mishandling the sacrifice, Christ incurred on behalf of all who deserved exclusion — he bore the iniquity that the covenant system threatened to lay on violators, taking it onto himself so that those who come to him 'will certainly not be cast out' (John 6:37).
9: A shadow: 'When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field to the very edge, and you shall not gather the gleanings after your harvest.' The gleaning law (Leviticus 19:9-10) is the legal provision for the poor and the alien — deliberate incompleteness as an act of structured generosity. Ruth gleans in Boaz's field under this provision (Ruth 2:2-3), and Boaz's generosity beyond the legal minimum prefigures Christ. Jesus feeds five thousand in the wilderness (John 6:1-14) — abundance with leftovers — and calls the church to share its resources so that 'there was not a needy person among them' (Acts 4:34).
10: A shadow: 'You shall not strip your vineyard bare, neither shall you gather the fallen grapes of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner.' The vineyard-gleaning law sets the scene for Jesus's parable of the vineyard (Matthew 21:33-43) — the owner who plants a vineyard and expects fruit from the tenants. The messianic vineyard motif runs from Isaiah 5 through John 15 ('I am the true vine'). Christ is the true vine owner who does not strip the vineyard bare but invites the poor and the alien into the harvest of the kingdom.
11: A shadow: 'You shall not steal; you shall not deal falsely; you shall not lie to one another.' Christ himself is 'the truth' (John 14:6) — the incarnate anti-lie, the one in whom there is no falsehood (1 Peter 2:22: 'no deceit was found in his mouth'). He fulfills the prohibition of deception not merely by observing the law but by being the ontological ground of truth. He also calls his followers to truthfulness: 'Let what you say be simply Yes or No' (Matthew 5:37). The kingdom he inaugurates is a community of truth because its head is the truth himself.
12: A shadow: 'You shall not swear by my name falsely, and so profane the name of your God.' Jesus intensifies this command: 'Do not take an oath at all... let what you say be Yes or No' (Matthew 5:34-37). Christ himself speaks with direct authority — 'Truly, truly I say to you' (Amen, amen legō hymin) rather than swearing by YHWH, because he is the one through whom YHWH speaks. He is 'the Amen, the faithful and true witness' (Revelation 3:14) — the one whose word needs no oath to validate it, who embodies the trustworthiness the oath-law was designed to protect.
13: A shadow: 'The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning.' The immediate payment law is the legal protection for the day-laborer — delay in wages is economic oppression. James 5:4 applies this directly: 'the wages of the laborers who mowed your fields, which you kept back by fraud, are crying out against you, and the cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord of hosts.' Christ identifies with the oppressed: 'I was hungry and you gave me food... as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me' (Matthew 25:35-40). The eschatological judge who hears the cries of the unpaid worker is the same Christ who himself labored for others.
14: A shadow: 'You shall not curse the deaf or put a stumbling block before the blind, but you shall fear your God.' Christ reverses the exclusion and danger that disability creates: he opens the ears of the deaf (Mark 7:32-35), gives sight to the blind (John 9), calls the crippled and lame to the messianic banquet (Luke 14:13). The law prohibited making the situation of the disabled worse; Christ makes it radically better. The law's protective intent points toward the kingdom where 'the blind receive their sight and the lame walk' (Matthew 11:5) — the messianic signs of the new age.
15: A shadow: 'You shall do no injustice in court. You shall not be partial to the poor or defer to the great, but in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor.' Christ as the righteous judge fulfills this perfectly: 'I can do nothing on my own. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is just, because I seek not my own will but the will of him who sent me' (John 5:30). He was himself subjected to the very injustice Leviticus 19:15 forbids — the chief priests and elders 'sought false testimony against Jesus' (Matthew 26:59), preferring the great (Roman power, temple establishment) over justice. The unjust trial of Christ exposes the law's violation; his resurrection is the Father's just verdict.
16: A shadow: 'You shall not go around as a slanderer among your people, and you shall not stand up against the life of your neighbor.' The prohibition against standing by while blood is shed becomes the negative type of Christ's positive act: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13). Christ does not stand by passively when his people face death — he intervenes, and the intervention costs him his own life. The law forbids indifference; the gospel shows the ultimate opposite of indifference: a God who enters the danger himself.
17: A shadow: 'You shall not hate your brother in your heart, but you shall reason frankly with your neighbor, lest you incur sin because of him.' Jesus builds on this: 'If your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault, between you and him alone' (Matthew 18:15) — the private rebuke as the first step of church discipline. Christ himself enacts this principle: he does not destroy sinners silently but calls them by name ('Zacchaeus, hurry and come down'), confronting sin in order to restore. The law requires honest confrontation; Christ models it as the basis of redemptive community.
19: A shadow: 'You shall keep my statutes. You shall not let your cattle breed with a different kind. You shall not sow your field with two kinds of seed, nor shall you wear a garment of cloth made of two kinds of material.' The kilayim (mixed-kinds) laws preserve created distinctions — each according to its kind, the created order of Gen 1. Paul applies the principle typologically: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (2 Corinthians 6:14) and develops a theology of the new creation community as distinct from the surrounding world. Christ himself is the one who draws the boundary: 'I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hates you' (John 15:19). The new covenant community is, like the holy garment and the unmingled field, a distinct kind.
20: A shadow: the law concerning a man who lies with a female slave betrothed to another — she shall not be put to death because she was not free; he brings a guilt offering. The gradations of culpability and the provision of atonement even in complex cases of sin point to the principle Christ embodies: no sin is beyond the reach of the guilt offering. Every case, however morally tangled, has a path to atonement. Christ is the guilt offering for the morally complex: 'he who did not know sin was made sin for us' (2 Corinthians 5:21), covering cases the Levitical system could not fully resolve.
21: A shadow: 'He shall bring his guilt offering to YHWH, to the entrance of the tent of meeting, a ram for a guilt offering.' The guilty party must come to the sanctuary to find atonement — the sin cannot be dealt with at a distance. Christ comes to where the sinner is: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14). But then he also brings the sinner to where God is: 'through him we have access in one Spirit to the Father' (Ephesians 2:18). The guilt offering's movement (sinner to sanctuary) is fulfilled in the double movement of the incarnation (God to sinner) and the ascension (sinner to God through Christ).
22: A shadow: 'And the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH with the ram of the guilt offering for his sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed.' The priestly mediation of forgiveness — the priest acts, the sacrifice is accepted, the sinner is forgiven — is the OT's enacted model of what Christ accomplishes once for all. 'He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2). The Levitical priest's mediation was real but limited to individual cases; Christ's mediation is universal and permanent.
23: A shadow: 'When you come into the land and plant any kind of tree for food, then you shall regard its fruit as forbidden. Three years it shall be forbidden to you; it must not be eaten.' The three-year prohibition before the fruit may be eaten patterns the waiting period before the harvest — appropriate time before the gift is received. The number resonates: Christ's public ministry lasted approximately three years before the fruit of his sacrifice was given. The waiting before the harvest is the shadow of the patience required in the redemptive plan: 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son' (Galatians 4:4).
24: A shadow: 'And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy, an offering of praise to YHWH.' The fourth-year fruit is consecrated to YHWH — the harvest dedicated before being enjoyed. Christ rises on the first day of the new week, the day of firstfruits — his resurrection-harvest is first consecrated to the Father ('I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God', John 20:17) before becoming the basis for the church's new life. The fourth-year consecration points to the resurrection dedication: Christ offered to the Father before being given to the church.
25: A shadow: 'And in the fifth year you may eat of its fruit, to increase its yield for you.' Only after dedication may the full harvest be enjoyed. Christ's gift to the church — the Spirit, the fullness of new covenant life — comes only after his consecration to the Father: 'Nevertheless, I tell you the truth: it is to your advantage that I go away, for if I do not go away, the Helper will not come to you' (John 16:7). The fifth-year abundance follows the fourth-year dedication; the Spirit's abundance in the church follows Christ's ascension and intercession at the Father's right hand.
26: A shadow: 'You shall not eat any flesh with blood in it. You shall not interpret omens or tell fortunes.' The prohibition of divination is the law's witness that the future belongs to YHWH alone. Christ fulfills this by being the one through whom God speaks his final word: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Hebrews 1:1-2). The need for oracles and omens ends when the Word himself comes. Christ also expels the unclean spirits that drove the divination practices of the ancient world: 'the prince of this world has been judged' (John 16:11).
27: A shadow: 'You shall not round off the hair on your temples or mar the edges of your beard.' These prohibitions ban the hair-cutting rites associated with pagan mourning practices — the body was not to be ritually disfigured as if death had ultimate power. Christ transforms mourning: 'Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted' (Matthew 5:4). His resurrection means death no longer has the last word, and the mourning-rites that expressed hopeless grief before pagan gods are replaced by the hope of the resurrection. 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25) — the one in whose presence pagan death-mourning is rendered obsolete.
28: A shadow: 'You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead or tattoo yourselves.' The prohibition against marking the body for the dead has an implicit NT counterpart: 'Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). The body belongs to God — in the OT because YHWH is Creator, in the NT because Christ's blood purchased it. The body is not a surface for ritual marking in the service of the dead; it is the Spirit's dwelling, purchased by the living Christ.
29: A shadow: 'Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute, lest the land fall into prostitution and the land become full of depravity.' The prohibition of sexual exploitation protects the daughter's honor. In Christ's fulfillment, the church is the daughter/bride who must be kept pure: 'I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ' (2 Corinthians 11:2). Christ 'gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle' (Ephesians 5:25-27). The law protects the daughter from prostitution; Christ dies to purify the bride.
30: A shadow: 'You shall keep my sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary: I am YHWH.' The sabbath and sanctuary command is fulfilled and transformed in Christ. Christ is 'Lord of the Sabbath' (Matthew 12:8), the one who gives true rest: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). The sanctuary (temple) is his body (John 2:21) and then the community of his Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16). The reverence due the physical sanctuary is now the reverence due the risen Christ who is present among his gathered people: 'where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them' (Matthew 18:20).
31: A shadow: 'Do not turn to mediums or necromancers; do not seek them out, and so make yourselves unclean by them.' The prohibition of consulting the dead for guidance is abolished in Christ, who is the one mediator: 'there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Timothy 2:5). All spiritual guidance comes through him and by his Spirit: 'When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all the truth' (John 16:13). The Witch of Endor consulted a dead spirit in despair (1 Sam 28); Christ himself is the living Lord who speaks to his people and gives them no cause to seek the dead.
32: A shadow: 'You shall stand up before the gray head and honor the face of an old man, and you shall fear your God.' The honor of the aged reflects YHWH's own eternity — wisdom accumulated over time reflects the character of the God who is from everlasting. Christ is the one in whom all wisdom and knowledge are hidden (Colossians 2:3) — not wisdom accumulated over time but wisdom that is eternal: 'before Abraham was, I am' (John 8:58). He is 'the Ancient of Days' (Daniel 7:9-13) in human form, the one before whom every knee will bow (Philippians 2:10) — the ultimate elder deserving honor.
33: A shadow: 'When a stranger sojourns with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong.' The protection of the alien is the ethical application of Israel's own experience: 'you were aliens in the land of Egypt.' Christ came to the alienated and the outsider as the one who was himself an outsider: 'the Word... came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). The alien-protection law finds its fullest expression in the Gentile mission: 'the Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have attained it' (Romans 9:30). The stranger who sojourns is now welcomed into the covenant family through Christ.
34: A direct quotation: 'You shall treat the stranger who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt.' This verse extends the neighbor-love command of Leviticus 19:18 explicitly to the alien — the love-your-neighbor command is not restricted to coethnics. Paul builds the entire Gentile-mission theology on this expansion: 'there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all' (Romans 10:12). Christ's death removes 'the dividing wall of hostility' (Ephesians 2:14) — the once-alien Gentiles are 'no longer strangers and aliens, but fellow citizens with the saints' (Ephesians 2:19).
35: A shadow: 'You shall do no wrong in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity.' Honest commercial dealings reflect YHWH's justice — a just God demands just measures from his people. Christ as the coming judge who will evaluate every weight and measure: 'with the judgment you pronounce you will be judged, and with the measure you use it will be measured to you' (Matthew 7:2). The eschatological judgment (Revelation 6:5-6 — the scales of judgment) renders every dishonest commercial transaction accountable. The just Judge who will weigh all things is the one whose own life was weighed and found to be of infinite worth.
36: A shadow: 'You shall have just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin.' The specific instruments of measurement — the stone weights, the dry measure, the liquid measure — are all subject to YHWH's justice. Christ is the 'faithful and true witness' (Revelation 3:14) and the 'cornerstone' (Ephesians 2:20) — the standard by which all things are measured. The church, called to honest measurement in commerce, is also called to 'speaking the truth in love' (Ephesians 4:15) — measuring all things by the standard of the one who is truth itself. 'Test everything; hold fast what is good' (1 Thessalonians 5:21).
37: A summary: 'You shall observe all my statutes and all my rules and do them: I am YHWH.' The Holiness Code closes with the inclusive summary — all the laws, both civil and religious, point to YHWH's character. Christ's declaration 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17) is the NT's direct response to Leviticus 19:37. He fulfills by embodying: the love-of-neighbor, the honest weights, the care for the alien, the purity of worship, the justice of judgment — Christ is the living summary of every statute in chapter 19.
